content.text: Devices are first-class identity objects in Microsoft Entra ID. When a device is registered or joined to Entra ID, it receives a device certificate and can participate in token issuance, Conditional Access evaluation, and Single Sign-On. Understanding the three device states is essential for designing Zero Trust access policies.

attrs.alt: Entra ID device registration states: Registered, Entra Joined, and Hybrid Entra Joined

content.text: The lightest touch — a user voluntarily registers a personal device (phone, tablet, personal laptop) by signing in to the Microsoft Authenticator app or Microsoft 365. The device gets a certificate issued by Entra ID. The organisation cannot manage or wipe this device through Intune unless the user separately enrolls in MDM.

The cloud-native model for corporate Windows 10/11 devices with no on-premises Active Directory requirement. During OOBE (Out Of Box Experience) or via Settings → Accounts, the device joins Entra ID and receives a Primary Refresh Token (PRT). The PRT enables SSO to every Entra ID-connected application without re-authentication. Intune can fully manage the device via MDM.

Devices already domain-joined to Active Directory DS are simultaneously registered in Entra ID via Entra Connect. They retain Kerberos authentication for on-premises resources while also getting Entra ID token issuance for cloud apps. This is the standard transition path for enterprises migrating from AD DS.

import requests

# List all devices in the tenant
url = "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/devices"
params = {
    "$select": "displayName,operatingSystem,operatingSystemVersion,trustType,isCompliant,isManaged,approximateLastSignInDateTime",
    "$top": 999
}
HDR = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"}
devices = []
while url:
    r = requests.get(url, headers=HDR, params=params)
    data = r.json()
    devices.extend(data.get("value", []))
    url = data.get("@odata.nextLink"); params = {}

# trustType values:
# "AzureAd"         = Entra Joined
# "ServerAd"        = Hybrid Entra Joined
# "Workplace"       = Entra Registered

stale_cutoff = "2025-12-01T00:00:00Z"  # 6+ months ago
stale = [d for d in devices if (d.get("approximateLastSignInDateTime") or "") < stale_cutoff]

print(f"Total devices: {len(devices)}")
print(f"Stale (>6 months no sign-in): {len(stale)}")
for d in stale[:10]:
    print(f"  {d['displayName']:<40} last: {d.get('approximateLastSignInDateTime','?')[:10]}")

import requests

USER_ID = "<user-object-id>"

# Devices registered or joined by a specific user
owned_devices = requests.get(
    f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/{USER_ID}/ownedDevices",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"},
    params={"$select": "displayName,operatingSystem,trustType,isCompliant,isManaged"}
).json().get("value", [])

print(f"Devices owned by user: {len(owned_devices)}")
for d in owned_devices:
    join_type = {"AzureAd": "Entra Joined", "ServerAd": "Hybrid", "Workplace": "Registered"}.get(d["trustType"], d["trustType"])
    print(f"  {d['displayName']:<35} {d['operatingSystem']:<10} {join_type:<15} compliant={d['isCompliant']} managed={d['isManaged']}")
Enabling Entra Join for New Devices
Entra Join is configured at tenant level. By default, all users can join devices. In enterprise environments, restrict this to specific groups and set a maximum device limit per user.

The most powerful use of device objects in Entra ID is as a signal in Conditional Access policies. You can require that a device be compliant (Intune-managed and meeting all compliance policies) before granting access to sensitive applications.

# CA policy device filter expressions (rule syntax)

# Match only Entra Joined corporate devices
device.isCompliant -eq True

# Match devices enrolled in Intune
device.isManaged -eq True

# Match specific device platforms
device.operatingSystem -in ["Windows", "macOS"]

# Match by trustType
device.trustType -eq "AzureAD"          # Entra Joined
device.trustType -eq "ServerAd"         # Hybrid Joined
device.trustType -eq "Workplace"        # Registered

# Exclude known kiosk device IDs
device.deviceId -notIn ["<kiosk-device-id-1>", "<kiosk-device-id-2>"]

# Complex example: require managed windows/mac, but allow mobile regardless
# Rule: (device.operatingSystem -in ["Windows","macOS"] and device.isManaged -eq true)
#       OR (device.operatingSystem -in ["iOS","Android"])

Stale devices accumulate in the directory over time — retired laptops, replaced phones, old VMs. Stale device objects represent audit noise and, more importantly, their associated tokens can still be valid until the PRT expires. Regular cleanup is a security hygiene requirement.

Windows Hello for Business (WHfB) replaces passwords on Entra Joined devices with strong two-factor authentication: biometric (face/fingerprint) or PIN backed by a device-bound cryptographic key pair. The private key never leaves the device's TPM.
Setup: during first sign-in on an Entra Joined device, the user registers a gesture (PIN, face, fingerprint)
Authentication: the device proves possession of the private key (TPM-bound); Entra ID verifies the public key and issues a PRT
Result: seamless SSO to all cloud apps with no password prompt — the user only sees their PIN or biometric prompt
Requirements: Windows 10/11, Entra Joined (or Hybrid Joined with P1), TPM 1.2 or 2.0 recommended
Conditional Access: can require WHfB by requiring 'multifactor authentication' and blocking 'password' auth method

# Verify WHfB registration status for a device
az rest --method GET     --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/<user-id>/authentication/windowsHelloForBusinessMethods"     --query "value[].{Id:id, CreatedAt:createdDateTime, KeyStrength:keyStrength}"

# WHfB key strength values: unknown | weak | normal
# normal = TPM-backed (required for compliance in most orgs)
Requiring 'compliant device' CA policy before Intune enrollment
Roll out Intune enrollment first; use report-only CA mode for 2 weeks
Deleting instead of disabling stale devices
Cannot restore a deleted device object; any active sessions using the device cert are immediately invalidated
Disable first (accountEnabled=false), wait 30 days, then delete
Hybrid Join not completing for some machines
Machines in the AD but not in Entra ID — CA device filter misses them
Check Entra Connect sync errors; verify SCP (Service Connection Point) in AD
Device limit per user too low
New device enrollment blocked for users who re-image or get a new laptop
Raise limit or create a cleanup policy to remove old device objects on offboarding

Windows Autopilot pre-stages device identities in Entra ID before the device ships to the user. When the user powers on the device and connects to the internet, Autopilot drives OOBE (Out-of-Box Experience) enrollment directly into Intune — no imaging required. Devices enrolled through Autopilot are Entra Joined by default.
Autopilot device identities are seeded via hardware hash (Get-WindowsAutoPilotInfo)
Group Tags on the Autopilot profile map to Intune enrollment profiles
User-Driven mode: user signs in and drives enrollment. Self-Deploying mode: no user interaction (shared devices, kiosks)
Autopilot reset re-enrols an existing device without reimaging — useful for device reassignment

# Enroll a Windows device using the Windows Enrollment API (Graph)
# Typically enrollment is initiated on the device itself, but you can
# pre-stage Autopilot device identities via Graph

import requests

TENANT_ID = "YOUR_TENANT_ID"
CLIENT_ID = "YOUR_APP_ID"
CLIENT_SECRET = "YOUR_SECRET"

def get_token():
    r = requests.post(
        f"https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token",
        data={
            "grant_type": "client_credentials",
            "client_id": CLIENT_ID,
            "client_secret": CLIENT_SECRET,
            "scope": "https://graph.microsoft.com/.default",
        }
    )
    return r.json()["access_token"]

token = get_token()
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}

# Import an Autopilot device hardware hash
# (hash comes from Get-WindowsAutoPilotInfo PowerShell script on device)
autopilot_payload = {
    "serialNumber": "DEVICE-SERIAL-123",
    "hardwareIdentifier": "<base64-hardware-hash>",
    "groupTag": "Sales-Laptops",
    "assignedUserPrincipalName": "alice@contoso.com"
}

resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/deviceManagement/importedWindowsAutopilotDeviceIdentities",
    headers=headers,
    json=autopilot_payload
)
print(resp.status_code, resp.json())
BitLocker Key Escrow via Entra ID

When a device is Entra Joined or Hybrid Entra Joined, BitLocker recovery keys are automatically escrowed to Entra ID (and optionally to Intune). Administrators can retrieve keys through the Azure portal or programmatically via Microsoft Graph. Key retrieval is audit-logged — every read is recorded.
Best practice: retrieve BitLocker keys only when actually needed for recovery. Each retrieval generates an audit event in Entra ID sign-in logs, providing full accountability.

# Retrieve BitLocker recovery keys for a device via Microsoft Graph
# Requires: BitLockerKey.Read.All (application permission)

import requests

def get_bitlocker_keys(token, device_id):
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
    # List all keys for a specific Azure AD device object ID
    resp = requests.get(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/informationProtection/bitlocker/recoveryKeys"
        f"?$filter=deviceId eq '{device_id}'&$select=id,createdDateTime,volumeType",
        headers=headers
    )
    keys = resp.json().get("value", [])
    return keys

def get_key_value(token, key_id):
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
    resp = requests.get(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/informationProtection/bitlocker/recoveryKeys/{key_id}"
        f"?$select=id,key",
        headers=headers
    )
    return resp.json().get("key")

token = get_token()  # from earlier example
device_id = "xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx"
keys = get_bitlocker_keys(token, device_id)
for k in keys:
    print(k["id"], k["volumeType"], k["createdDateTime"])
    # Only retrieve the key value if you actually need it (audit-logged)
    # actual_key = get_key_value(token, k["id"])
AD Users and Computers > Computer > BitLocker

Device compliance policies define the minimum security baseline a device must meet before Conditional Access grants resource access. Compliance is evaluated by Intune and the result (compliant/non-compliant) is communicated to Entra ID. Conditional Access can then gate access on the compliance state.
Compliance policies are platform-specific: Windows, macOS, iOS, Android each have distinct settings
Non-compliance grace period: configurable window before access is blocked (default 0 days = immediate)
Actions for non-compliance: send email, remotely lock, retire/wipe device
Custom compliance: upload a PowerShell script that outputs JSON — Intune evaluates it against your rules

# PowerShell: Create a compliance policy in Intune (Microsoft Graph)
# Requires: DeviceManagementConfiguration.ReadWrite.All

$token = (Get-AzAccessToken -ResourceUrl "https://graph.microsoft.com").Token
$headers = @{ Authorization = "Bearer $token"; "Content-Type" = "application/json" }

$policy = @{
    "@odata.type" = "#microsoft.graph.windows10CompliancePolicy"
    displayName   = "Windows 10 Baseline Compliance"
    description   = "Requires BitLocker, Secure Boot, and OS minimum version"
    # OS version gate
    osMinimumVersion = "10.0.19041"    # Windows 10 2004
    osMaximumVersion = ""
    # Security settings
    bitLockerEnabled            = $true
    secureBootEnabled           = $true
    codeIntegrityEnabled        = $true
    storageRequireEncryption    = $true
    # Microsoft Defender
    defenderEnabled             = $true
    defenderVersion             = ""
    signatureOutOfDate          = $false
    rtpEnabled                  = $true    # real-time protection
    antivirusRequired           = $true
    antiSpywareRequired         = $true
    # Password
    passwordRequired            = $true
    passwordMinimumLength       = 8
    passwordRequiredType        = "deviceDefault"
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5

$response = Invoke-RestMethod `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/deviceManagement/deviceCompliancePolicies" `
    -Method POST `
    -Headers $headers `
    -Body $policy
Write-Output "Created policy: $($response.id)"

# Assign the policy to a group
$assignment = @{
    assignments = @(@{
        target = @{
            "@odata.type" = "#microsoft.graph.groupAssignmentTarget"
            groupId = "YOUR-GROUP-OBJECT-ID"
        }
    })
} | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 4

Invoke-RestMethod `
    -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/deviceManagement/deviceCompliancePolicies/$($response.id)/assign" `
    -Method POST `
    -Headers $headers `
    -Body $assignment

Once compliance policies are in place, Conditional Access can enforce them as a grant control. The policy can require either a compliant device (Intune-managed) or a Hybrid Entra Joined device — or both. Always test in report-only mode before enabling enforcement.
Auditing Device Trust and Compliance State

Regularly auditing the relationship between Entra ID device objects and their Intune compliance state surfaces devices that have drifted out of compliance, been unenrolled from MDM, or lost their Entra Join status. The following script cross-references Intune managed devices with their compliance state.

# Query device trust state and compliance status together via Graph
# Useful for auditing which devices are trusted vs compliant

import requests

def audit_device_trust(token):
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}
    # managedDevices lives in Intune (DeviceManagement namespace)
    resp = requests.get(
        "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/deviceManagement/managedDevices"
        "?$select=deviceName,operatingSystem,complianceState,azureADDeviceId,"
        "joinType,enrolledDateTime,lastSyncDateTime"
        "&$top=100",
        headers=headers
    )
    devices = resp.json().get("value", [])
    
    summary = {"compliant": [], "noncompliant": [], "unknown": [], "inGracePeriod": []}
    for d in devices:
        state = d.get("complianceState", "unknown")
        summary.setdefault(state, []).append(d["deviceName"])
    
    for state, names in summary.items():
        print(f"{state}: {len(names)} devices")
    
    # Cross-reference with Entra ID device object for trustType
    noncompliant = [d for d in devices if d.get("complianceState") == "noncompliant"]
    print(f"\nNon-compliant devices ({len(noncompliant)}):")
    for d in noncompliant[:10]:
        print(f"  {d['deviceName']} | last sync: {d.get('lastSyncDateTime','never')}")
    return devices

token = get_token()
audit_device_trust(token)

Entra ID devices are identity objects that enable device-based Conditional Access, Windows Hello for Business, and SSO via Primary Refresh Tokens. The three join states (Registered, Entra Joined, Hybrid Joined) represent increasing levels of management and trust.
Entra Registered: BYOD, partial SSO, no Intune MDM — lowest trust signal for CA policies
Entra Joined: cloud-native corporate devices, full SSO via PRT, full Intune management — recommended for new deployments
Hybrid Joined: existing AD DS-managed PCs also registered in Entra ID — transition state for AD-to-cloud migrations
Use Conditional Access device filters to require compliant or joined devices for sensitive applications
Clean up stale devices regularly: disable first, delete after 30 days; a stale device with an active PRT is a security risk
Windows Hello for Business on Entra Joined devices provides phishing-resistant passwordless authentication backed by TPM
